problem_addressed: Humanoid robots face perceptual limitations with narrow fields of view and self-occlusion that constrain effective workspace in unstructured environments.
core_claim: OmniDP enables robust large-workspace humanoid manipulation through omnidirectional 3D perception using an end-to-end LiDAR-driven visuomotor policy.
key_contributions: Development of OmniDP, an end-to-end LiDAR-driven 3D visuomotor policy for large-workspace manipulation
Time-Aware Attention Pooling mechanism for processing sparse and temporally flickering LiDAR point clouds
Whole-body teleoperation system for efficient demonstration data collection on full-body humanoid robots
Demonstration of superior performance in large-workspace and cluttered scenarios compared to RGB-D baselines
novelty: First application of panoramic LiDAR perception specifically for humanoid manipulation tasks requiring large workspace operation, unlike prior LiDAR work focused on locomotion/navigation.
method_summary: OmniDP processes panoramic LiDAR point clouds through a pyramid convolutional encoder enhanced with time-aware attention, producing features that condition a Diffusion Policy decoder for action generation, enabling 360° workspace awareness without base repositioning.
method_components: Panoramic LiDAR perception system (Livox MID-360) providing 360° horizontal and -7° to 52° vertical FOV
Time-Aware Attention Pooling mechanism aggregating point clouds over temporal windows with weighted recent observations
Point cloud preprocessing pipeline cropping points beyond 1.3m and downsampling to 4096 points
Diffusion Policy decoder generating coordinated upper-body actions conditioned on global features and joint states
key_innovation: Time-Aware Attention Pooling mechanism that stabilizes perception by aggregating sparse LiDAR data over temporal windows while emphasizing recent observations, enabling robust processing of flickering point clouds for policy learning.
assumptions: Objects within 1.3m of robot are most relevant for manipulation tasks
Proprioceptive joint states (43-dim) are available for full-body coordination
Lower body is controlled separately by pre-trained HOMIE algorithm
LiDAR point clouds are represented in egocentric frame eliminating calibration needs
theoretical_grounding: No formal theoretical proofs presented; performance validated through extensive simulation and real-world experiments demonstrating superior success rates in large-workspace and cluttered scenarios.
datasets_used: Teleoperated LiDAR point clouds + proprioceptive joint positions (29 DoFs)
hyperparameters: 20 trials per task under consistent initial conditions
headline_result: OmniDP achieved 82/120 total success rate (16/20 Pick & Place, 12/20 Pour OV, 15/20 Pick & Place real, 12/20 Hand Over OV, 11/20 Wipe OV)
improvement_over_baseline: 44% absolute improvement in overall success rate (82/120 vs 25/120 best baseline) and 7x reduction in collision rate (5/20 vs 18/20)
stated_limitations: Existing approaches to expanding perception introduce mechanical complexity, calibration dependencies, and latency that hinder reliable real-time performance.
unstated_weaknesses: The paper focuses on humanoid robot manipulation, which is fundamentally different from medical image classification.
The use of LiDAR for perception is not directly applicable to medical imaging modalities.
The paper doesn't address specific challenges of medical image classification such as handling different imaging modalities or class imbalance.
The evaluation metrics and datasets used for robot manipulation may not be relevant to medical image classification.
threats_to_validity: The paper's focus on robotics manipulation may not generalize to medical image classification tasks.
The challenges of perception in robotics (like self-occlusion) are fundamentally different from those in medical imaging.
The paper doesn't provide evidence that the approach would work in medical imaging scenarios.
reproducibility: medium (no code mentioned, complex system with LiDAR data)
open_questions: How can the calibration-aware approach from this robotics paper be adapted to medical image classification?
What are the specific calibration challenges in medical imaging that are similar to those in robotics?
Can the active learning principles from this paper be applied to medical image classification tasks?
future_work: Expanding the approach to more complex manipulation tasks.
Exploring integration of other sensing modalities.
gaps_this_enables: The paper highlights the importance of calibration in perception systems, which could inspire calibration-aware approaches in medical image classification.
The focus on omnidirectional awareness could lead to more comprehensive medical image analysis systems.
The active learning aspects could be adapted to medical image classification, where labeling is expensive and time-consuming.
relevance_to_topic: The paper's focus on calibration dependencies in perception systems is tangentially relevant to calibration-aware active learning for medical image classification, as both domains face challenges with accurate calibration.
reusable_assets: Methodology for handling calibration in perception systems
best_result: OmniDP achieved 82/120 total success rate (16/20 Pick & Place, 12/20 Pour OV, 15/20 Pick & Place real, 12/20 Hand Over OV, 11/20 Wipe OV)
limitations_stated: Existing approaches to expanding perception introduce mechanical complexity, calibration dependencies, and latency that hinder reliable real-time performance.
title: OmniDP: Beyond-FOV Large-Workspace Humanoid Manipulation with Omnidirectional 3D Perception
